next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 16

The Lincoln Theatre was on East Long Street, in a part of Columbus that Doug had driven past for twenty-six years without ever registering as a place where people went on purpose. He'd processed a claim once for a storefront two blocks east — water intrusion, failed flashing, four inches of standing water in a basement that had been a jazz bar in the 1940s — but the theater itself had never entered his consciousness until Layla sent him a one-page fact sheet listing its seating capacity (582 on her sheet; 580 in the Netflix contract, so her sheet was wrong and Doug had not raised it), its Art Deco heritage, and three bullet points under the heading "Why This Venue Works For Your Brand."

He had never been inside it. He was inside it now.

The lobby smelled like old carpet and fresh paint. Black cables ran from the front doors to the theater entrance under strips of gaffer tape. Doug had nearly tripped on the first strip and learned the name from a crew member. Gray adhesive residue clung to the edge of his shoe. He noticed things carefully and for no useful purpose.

The stage was bigger than he'd imagined. He'd pictured something the size of the comedy clubs — Cleveland, Pittsburgh, Detroit — where the stages were platforms, barely elevated, close enough to the front row that you could see the drinks sweating. This was different. The stage at the Lincoln Theatre was wide and deep, with a proscenium arch above it and wings on either side where things disappeared into darkness, and when Doug walked out onto it for the first time, at 10:15 a.m. on a Wednesday in...
open_hooks_to_carry: Dinner at a bar in the Short North — not a date, not a session, the professional structure exhausted
Simone's backstory: a performer who moved to writing because other people's voices were easier than her own
prev_tail_excerpt:
"A guy spent half the set yelling for the toast."

"And?"

"I called him sir."

Simone made a sound through her nose that might have been a laugh. "Jesus."

"Then I talked about hotel coffee and parking lots and they listened."

"Listened how?"

"Like they wanted the report, not the brochure."

The line went quiet except for highway noise under it.

"What we built doesn't work," Doug said.

"I know."

"The set list's a stack of forms. I can organize it, label it, read every line in order, and the room still knows I'm filing paperwork instead of telling them what happened."

"The taping's in two weeks."

He waited for the part where she would tell him what to do next. She did not offer it.

"Get home safe," she said. "Tuesday we'll look at what was alive."

He got to Clintonville a little after two. The apartment was dark. He let himself in, set his keys beside Simone's pen cap on the kitchen counter, and looked at the yellow Cheerios box without opening it. In two weeks he would stand at the Lincoln Theatre in front of 580 people and cameras. He turned off the kitchen light and left the cereal where it was.
state_deltas_required: The approaching taping and the exhaustion of every scripted option — the only thing left is the truth, which is the thing Doug is most afraid of
Doug understands what the toast was — truth delivered without the safety net of intention — and faces the impossible koan of being unintentional on purpose
